utterances: I think people are very, very upset.

More than 30 or 40,000 people marched against, you know, the federal control board, who's overseeing the island's finances, that recently approved a fiscal plan that outlines, you know, these measures that the governor has been touting.
Everybody or most everybody understands that there's a need for change.
But the anger here is toward previous administrations that have built this debt up over decades and decades.
And now many feel that they are paying for this.
And obviously, they don't like it.
There's been marches.
There's more marches planned.
And I think Puerto Ricans here have hit their limit for changes.
That's Danica Coto speaking with us via Skype.
She's a Caribbean-based reporter with the Associated Press.
Thank you for speaking with us.
Thank you very much for having me.
Now to the West Bank and a Jewish settlement with ties to members of the Trump administration.
The Trump White House has broken from decades of U.S.
policy on settlements by saying that they do not impede peace between Israelis and Palestinians.
NPR's Joanna Kakissis has the story.
Palestinian Jabar Mousa was 15 when he first met the Jewish settlers who would move in next to his village of Dura al-Qar'.
It was 1977.
(Through interpreter) We stop and ask these young men - who are you?
And they said, we are students of the yeshiva.
We study in a school in Beit El.
Beit El is Hebrew for House of God.
The yeshiva students believed they were on the site where the biblical Jacob dreamt of angels going up a ladder to heaven.
(Foreign language spoken).
(SOUNDBITE OF FOOTSTEPS)
Today, just down the hill from that site, Israeli Chaim Silberstein greets us at his office in Beit El's town hall.
I'm an idealist.
I moved to Israel from South Africa 37 years ago.
I always wanted to be an active part of the Zionist dream.
When he moved here, 150 families lived in Beit El.
Now there are more than 1,200 - nearly 7,000 people.
He says they're here because God promised Jacob the land of Israel.
So this is considered a holy area, a holy site.
And so therefore, the return to Beit El is considered a return to 4,000 years of Jewish history.
Beit El is a slice of Jewish Orthodox suburbia.
It has clinics, a steak house, 20 kindergartens and a roundabout with sculptures of pomegranates, figs and grapes.
Since Donald Trump was inaugurated, Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has approved more than 3,000 new settler homes, at least 20 here.
The greatest smell for people that want to build is the smell of mud and dust and earth.
And I believe within the next few months, possibly, we could begin to see some bulldozers working in Beit El.
The American Friends of Beit El Yeshiva has raised millions of dollars for the religious seminary here.
A fundraiser for the group is David Friedman, president Trump's pick for ambassador to Israel.
Trump's son-in-law, Jared Kushner, and Trump himself have also donated.
The yeshivas prestige also brings business to Avraham Babad.
His family makes to tefillin - small containers with parchment scrolls of handwritten Torah verses.
Jewish men tie them to their heads during morning prayers.
Babad says David Friedman bought one here.
(Through interpreter) Whoever can help us live what's in the scriptures and fulfill the promise of this land - that's very good.
But what's been good for Beit El has not been good for everyone.
Israel captured the West Bank from Jordan in 1967.
The U.N.
considers settlements illegal, and Beit El sits right in the middle of the West Bank - one reason why Palestinians see settlements here as a threat to their independent state.
Beit El includes land that used to be farmed by Palestinians.
Lines of cars coming in, parking lots filling up, the engines starting.
You'd have the smells of all the vendors and the food carts.
And there would just be a buzz of energy in the air.
Right now, it's obviously not that.
Thursday's cancellation comes after another hit, earlier this year: This spring, the sequestration budget cuts also threatened to shut down the air show.
But organizers found a way to salvage it.
They planned to put on a scaled-down show, headlined by civilian flyers.
Then came the shutdown, which scrapped the whole thing.
It all happened so quickly, many in San Diego still didn't know, like Rhaiza Jablecki.
I actuality broke the news about Miramar to her.
That's sad.
This is our tradition.
Jablecki says she used to take her sons to the show.
What she'll miss the most...
Going inside the planes.
Some of the big carriers.
My boys love to jump inside, and take pictures and just going inside of the actual planes.
Jablecki says the shutdown might hurt her bottom line, too.
Her husband is a civilian, but his employer does a lot of work with the military.
Congressman Scott Peters' House district covers Miramar.
And Camp Pendleton sits just to the north.
Peters says a big chunk of San Diego's economy is defense.
The San Diego economy has three main drivers.
One's tourism, one's innovation and science and technology, and one's the military.
The military accounts for about 25 percent of our jobs.
And apart from the military, government workers are also being hurt.
San Diego's one of the top ten places for federal employees.
We've seen about 30,000 layoffs.
It's having a huge impact on us.
Military towns like San Diego will get some relief from this latest hit.
The Pentagon announced Saturday that it's ordering most of its civilian employees furloughed by the shutdown back to work.
Rhaiza Jablecki hopes the whole shutdown doesn't go on much longer.
I hope no more than this weekend.
And until it's resolved, she recommends some tough medicine for Capitol Hill.
Congress should not get paid until they figure this out.
It's a message echoed throughout the entire city.
Washington needs to figure it out.
Because in places like San Diego - all across the country - more than just an air show is at stake.
Sam Sanders, NPR News.
Today News and Notes begin our fitness series.
For the next few months, I'll be taking you through my quest to get healthy.
That means eating better, finding ways to exercise that I actually enjoy, and getting medical advice on how to live healthy.
According to the Food and Drug Administration, more than 60 percent of Americans are overweight, and well, I'm one of them.
So as I work to get in shape and stay there, I'm going to take you with me.
First stop, the LA Acura bike tour, which follows the course of the Los Angeles marathon before the runners get started.
It's 5 a.m., and thousands of people are lined up with their bikes along Los Angeles's Exposition Boulevard.
Some people are here to test themselves.
Some are here to bond with loved ones.
I am here to make a promise to myself that in the next year I will transform myself physically and mentally, and really take care of my body.
I'm not the only one on a mission.
Janet Cormier of Englewood, California is motivated.
Well, the excitement of the race, this is my first time doing it, and I'm very excited about it.
What convinced you that you could do this?
Are you a long time bike rider?
No. I actually just set a goal for myself and I wanted to do discipline myself to actually do it.
So I'm very excited that I made it out here today and I'm actually meeting my goal.
And Raj Batra of Beverly Hills made this ride a family affair, complete with matching T-shirts.
I'm with my friend Sherman Sharma(ph) and his boys, and my son.
So what made you think this would be a great thing to do together as a group?
It was his idea.
Is that blame or a compliment?
No, it's a compliment.
I'm so glad he got us out here.
We hope that hopefully, in a couple of years from now, they're the ones who are going to be, hey, come on dad, we got to go ride the marathon.
Can you introduce us to your son?
Sure.
This is Chanan(ph) Batra.
Hi, Chanan.
Why is this a good idea for you?
